text: The laparoscopic type C-I radical hysterectomy.
The surgeon begins the procedure with a left pelvic lymphadenectomy, using laparoscopic instruments to carefully remove lymphatic tissue from the left pelvic region.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to clear adhesions and improve exposure of the left pelvic area, facilitating access for the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon coagulates and cuts the left round ligament using laparoscopic instruments, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision both cranially and caudally, increasing the surgical field exposure.
The surgeon grasps the vesico-umbilical artery and pulls it medially, exposing the entrance to the paravesical space during the laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon dissects the paravesical space, extending the dissection down to the pelvic floor, revealing the anatomical structures within this area.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the vesico-hypogastric fascia, carefully separating the fascia from surrounding tissues in the pelvic area.
The surgeon dissects caudally from the levator ani muscle towards the bifurcation of the iliac artery, carefully navigating the anatomical structures in the pelvic region.
The surgeon identifies the internal iliac artery and vesico-hypogastric fascia as the medial limits of the dissection, with these anatomical structures clearly visible within the surgical field.
The ureter is visualized medial to the internal iliac artery during the dissection.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the ventral and medial limits of the paravesical space, then shifts laterally to detach the external iliac vessels from the underlying psoas muscle.
The surgeon performs dissection over the fascia of the psoas muscle, continuing the lateral approach after detaching the external iliac vessels.
The surgeon detaches the paravisceral fat from its medial and lateral borders, then dissects it away from the external iliac vessels and the parietal branches of the internal iliac vessels.
The genitofemoral nerve is visualized and preserved during the dissection.
The surgeon removes lymphatic and fatty tissue circumferentially around the external iliac vessels, ensuring complete clearance of these structures.
The surgeon meticulously dissects the variable anatomy of the parietal branches of the internal iliac artery and vein in the prespinous region, carefully navigating around these structures.
The surgeon performs a right pelvic lymphadenectomy, carefully dissecting lymphatic tissue from the surrounding anatomical structures in the pelvic region.
The surgeon removes the right pelvic lymph nodes using meticulous dissection techniques, similar to those used for the lymphatic and fatty tissue around the external iliac vessels.
The surgeon develops the vesicovaginal space, carefully dissecting between the bladder and the vagina to create a clear surgical plane.
The surgeon coagulates and cuts the peritoneum of the vesicovaginal fold from left to right, extending towards the round ligaments.
The surgeon uses atraumatic forceps to grasp the urinary bladder and gently pulls it upwards, creating tension in the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the urinary bladder from the cervix and proximal vagina, fully developing the vesicovaginal space up to the level of the vesicle trigone.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection, developing the Yabuki fort spaces adjacent to the previously dissected vesicovaginal space.
The surgeon visualizes the distal ureters, which are exposed following the development of the vesicovaginal space and dissection of surrounding tissues.
The surgeon develops the right Latsko space, continuing the dissection around the vesicovaginal area, with visible separation of tissue layers.
In the nerve-sparing hysterectomy procedure, the surgeon carefully dissects the Latsko space to a limited extent, ensuring the pelvic plexus remains undamaged.
The surgeon removes the lymphatic tissue located medially to the internal iliac artery, carefully dissecting around this vascular structure.
The surgeon isolates the right vesico-umbilical artery, visually separating it from surrounding tissues within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the vesico-umbilical artery from the ventral parametrum, followed by coagulation and cutting of the superficial vesicle artery.
The surgeon manages the right lateral parametrium, focusing on careful dissection and manipulation of the tissue in this area.
The surgeon coagulates and cuts the uterine artery near the internal iliac artery, ensuring precise separation of the vessel from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon coagulates and cuts the superficial uterine vein, completing the dissection of vascular structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon develops the right Okabayashi space, carefully dissecting and separating tissue layers to create a clear surgical field in the pelvic region.
The surgeon has extensively developed the Okabayashi space, dissecting the mesoureter and fibers of the hypogastric nerve away from the dorsal parametrium.

The surgeon isolates the right deep uterine vein, located medial to the ureter, and proceeds to coagulate and cut the vein, ensuring no mechanical or thermal damage occurs to the surrounding pelvic plexus.
The video shows the spared branches of the pelvic plexus, which are visibly intact and undisturbed during the surgical procedure.
The surgeon performs ureter unroofing and manages the ventral parametrium on the right side, carefully exposing and manipulating these structures during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon coagulates and cuts the ureteral branch of the uterine artery, allowing safe entry into the ureteric canal.
The surgeon carefully cuts the superficial layer of the vesicle uterine ligament near the bladder wall, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
After cutting the vesicouterine ligament, the surgeon lateralizes the distal ureter, exposing the medial portion of the deep layer of the vesicouterine ligament.
The surgeon visualizes the bladder branch of the pelvic splanchnic nerve, highlighting its location relative to the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon isolates the left vesico-umbilical artery, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues within the surgical field.
The surgeon detaches the left vesico-umbilical artery from the ventral parametria, then coagulates and cuts the left superior vesicle artery.
The surgeon manages the left lateral parametria, focusing on the anatomical structures and ensuring proper separation and exposure of the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon coagulates and cuts the left uterine artery and the superficial uterine vein, completing the vascular control in this area.
The surgeon develops the left Okabayashi space, carefully dissecting and separating the surrounding tissues to create a clear surgical field.
The surgeon completes the development of the left Okabayashi space, pushing the mesouriter and hypogastric nerve laterally to create a clear surgical field.
The surgeon performs ureter unroofing and manages the ventral parametrium on the left side, carefully exposing and manipulating the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon grasps the cut end of the uterine vessels and pulls them laterally, revealing the surrounding tissue structures and enhancing exposure for further dissection.
The surgeon cuts the superficial layer of the vesicle uterine ligament near the bladder wall, carefully separating the ligament from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon visually inspects the course of the ureter, ensuring its position and integrity within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects the ureter from the surrounding tissue, separating it from adjacent anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully maintains the integrity of the ureteral sheath to minimize devascularization of the distal ureter during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the ureter up to its entry into the urinary bladder, ensuring the ureter is clearly separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon manages the dorsal parametria, performing a posterior parametrial resection with nerve-sparing technique, as the hypogastric nerves have been completely detached and lateralized.
The surgeon begins resection at the rectosigmoid colon, carefully progressing towards the vaginal area, ensuring precise separation of tissues.
The surgeon identifies the embryological plane between the mesorectum and dorsal parametrium, carefully delineating these anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses the Harmonic ACE device to perform precise dissection, likely focusing on maintaining tissue integrity and minimizing bleeding.
After dissecting and lateralizing both dorsal parametria from the mesorectal envelope, the surgeon uses atraumatic forceps to grasp the rectum and pull it cranially.
The surgeon identifies the rectovaginal plane and safely dissects the rectum away from the dorsal vagina, ensuring clear separation between these anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs radical resection of the dorsal parametria, working close to the rectum, using precise dissection techniques.
Management of paraculpia.

The surgeon uses the harmonic ACE device to resect the paraculpia along with the medial vascular portion of the deep layer of the vesico-cervical ligament, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs a colpotomy, creating an incision in the vaginal wall to facilitate access to the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon excises the uterus along with the proximal portion of the vagina, completing the removal in a standard surgical fashion.
The surgeon uses a ceramic colpotomizer attached to the Clermont-Ferrand uterine manipulator to facilitate the procedure, allowing for precise adjustment of the excised vaginal length.
The surgeon performs dissection of the common iliac and subaortic lymph nodes, carefully isolating these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the common iliac and subaortic lymph nodes, marking the final step of the procedure.
The surgeon moves the laparoscopic scope from the standard umbilical port to the supraumbilical port, enhancing visualization of the surgical field.
The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision cranially towards the superior mesenteric artery, revealing deeper anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies the right and left ureters as lateral landmarks during the lymphadenectomy, ensuring precise dissection around these anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs peritoneal suspension, adjusting the peritoneal tissue to enhance exposure and access to the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a bursing needle to suspend the edges of the dissected peritoneum, fully exposing the area designated for lymphadenectomy.
The surgeon begins dissection over the common iliac arteries, with the area fully exposed for lymphadenectomy following peritoneal suspension and visualization adjustments.
The surgeon isolates and spares the superior hypogastric plexus, ensuring it remains intact during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects fatty and lymphatic tissue around the aortic bifurcation, ensuring the superior hypogastric plexus and proximal hypogastric nerves remain undamaged.
The surgeon dissects the common iliac, proximal para-aortic, and paracaval lymph nodes, with the peritoneum suspended to fully expose the area for lymphadenectomy.
The surgeon performs vaginal closure, suturing the vaginal cuff following the completion of the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon closes the vaginal cuff using two figure-of-eight sutures with 2-0 absorbable intracorporeal sutures, securing the tissue edges together.

The final view shows the paravisceral space cleared of lymphatic and fatty tissue, revealing the obturator nerve, parietal branch of internal iliac vessels, ventral part of the sciatic nerve, intrapelvic ischial spine, and sacrospinous ligament.
This specimen.
The image shows the resected proximal vagina along with the dorsal and ventrolateral parametria, all removed in a single block.
